title: SEGMENT 7 — Paired-end reads and insert size: what they enable

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Paired-end reads and insert size: what they enable
So far I've described the sequencing cycle as if it just runs one direction along the fragment, from the adapter inward, producing a single read of some length — call it 150 base pairs.
That's called a single-end read.
It's perfectly fine for some applications.

But almost every serious Illumina sequencing project today does something more clever: after finishing the first read in one direction, the instrument actually goes back and reads the same fragment from the other end, producing a second read.
We call this paired-end sequencing, and the two reads from the same fragment are called the "mate pair" or just "the pair.
Here's the mechanics.
After completing the first 150-cycle read from the forward adapter, the instrument performs another short series of operations on the cluster.

It does what's called a read 2 turnaround: it resynthesizes the cluster from the other primer, doing a kind of mini-bridge amplification step to regenerate the strand starting from the opposite anchor, then attaches a new sequencing primer that's complementary to the other adapter.
Now, when the next round of incorporate-image-cleave-repeat starts, the polymerase begins at the other end of the original fragment and reads back toward the middle.
Another 150 cycles, another 150 base pairs of sequence, but this time from the opposite end of the same fragment that produced the first read.
So at the end, every cluster gives you not one read but two — a forward read of 150 bp and a reverse read of 150 bp — both derived from the same original fragment.
We call them Read 1 and Read 2.
They are linked.
We know they came from the same fragment because they came from the same cluster on the flow cell, and the instrument keeps track of that linkage.
Why do this?
Why pay the time cost of running essentially two sequencing runs on the same flow cell?
Three reasons, and they're worth understanding because they shape almost everything about how Illumina data is used downstream.
Reason one: insert size as a constraint for mapping.

If your library was prepared so that the original fragments were about 400 base pairs long, and you read 150 bp from each end, then you know — for every read pair — that the two reads should map back to the genome roughly 400 base pairs apart, with the forward read on one strand and the reverse read on the other strand.
That distance and orientation constraint is enormously useful for the bioinformatics step.

When you're aligning short reads back to a reference genome and you hit ambiguous regions — places where the reference has repeated sequences, places where the read could plausibly map to several locations — the paired-end constraint disambiguates.
Among the possible mapping locations for Read 1, the right one is almost always the one where Read 2 also maps to a location about 400 bp away with the correct orientation.
The pair-mate-distance becomes a check that resolves ambiguity.
Without paired-end reads, you'd lose a substantial fraction of your mapped data to repeat-region ambiguity.
Reason two: detecting structural variants.

Sometimes when you map a paired-end library, you find a read pair where Read 1 and Read 2 map to the genome, but with the wrong distance between them — way too far apart, or way too close, or on completely different chromosomes.
Those discordant pairs are biological signal.

They tell you that the original DNA molecule had a structural rearrangement compared to the reference — a deletion (which makes the apparent insert size larger than expected), an insertion (which makes it smaller), an inversion, or a translocation between chromosomes.
The whole field of structural variant detection in cancer genomics, in rare disease, in population studies, relies on this signal.
A single discordant read pair is just noise.
A cluster of dozens of discordant pairs all suggesting the same rearrangement is a structural variant call.
Paired-end sequencing is what makes this kind of analysis possible.
Reason three: better quality at the fragment ends.
Illumina sequencing tends to be slightly less accurate at the ends of reads — particularly the 3-prime end, the part read last.
By reading from both ends, you get the high-quality early bases of Read 1 at one end of the fragment, and the high-quality early bases of Read 2 at the other end.
The middle of the fragment is more reliably called.

For variant calling in particular, this matters: a paired-end run with 150-bp reads from a 400-bp fragment gives you very high-confidence reads at both ends, with overlap or near-overlap in the middle.
Here's the analogy I want you to hold.
Paired-end reads are like reading both ends of a long sentence.
You don't see the middle, but knowing the beginning and end gives you a strong constraint on what the sentence is and how long it is.

If a sentence on the original page started with "The quick brown fox" and ended with "over the lazy dog," and you know it was a sentence of roughly that span, then you can place it in the original book even if you can't read the middle.
Single-end reads give you the beginning of every sentence.
Paired-end reads give you the beginning and the end, with the distance between them as a verification.
In modern practice, almost all Illumina sequencing is done paired-end.

Notation: a run described as "2 by 150" means paired-end with 150 bp from each end — so 300 bp of sequence per fragment, with a gap of unknown sequence in the middle if your insert is longer than 300 bp.
A run described as "2 by 75" is paired-end with 75 bp reads from each end.

Single-end runs are still done for some applications — small RNA sequencing, certain types of expression profiling, very low-depth survey sequencing — but for genomics, exome sequencing, structural variant detection, anything where you care about precision and completeness, paired-end is the default.
One more practical detail.
The choice of insert size matters and depends on what you want to do.

For variant calling and de novo assembly, you usually want inserts in the 350-to-500 base pair range — long enough to bridge most short repeats but short enough that the fragments stay intact through library prep and amplification.
For applications like ChIP-seq, where the input DNA is already fragmented by the experimental protocol, the insert size is determined by the biology and is typically shorter, maybe 200 base pairs.

For special applications like mate-pair libraries — which are an older technique for spanning long repeats — you can construct libraries with much larger effective insert sizes, but we won't get into that today.
The headline is: paired-end is standard, insert size is something you choose during library prep, and the pair-mate-distance is one of the most useful pieces of information in your data.
